claim: For all integers n, n^5 is congruent to n modulo 10.
For all integers n, n^5 - n is divisible by 5.
For all integers n, n^5 - n is even.
For all integers n, n^3 - n is divisible by 3.
For all integers n, n^5 - n is divisible by 30.
The product (n-1)n(n+1)(n^2+1) is always a multiple of 30.
If n is not divisible by 5, then n^4 - 1 is divisible by 5.
If n is not divisible by 3, then n^2 - 1 is divisible by 3.
If n is odd, n^2 - 1 is divisible by 8.
If n is coprime to 30, then n^4 - 1 is divisible by 120.
The last digit of n^5 is always equal to the last digit of n.
The last digit of n^9 is always equal to the last digit of n.
For all integers n, n^9 - n^5 is divisible by 10.
For all n, n^5(n^4 - 1) is divisible by 10.
For all integers n, n^7 - n is divisible by 7.
For all integers n, n^7 - n is divisible by 210.
The product (n-1)n(n+1)(n^4+n^2+1) is always a multiple of 210.
The greatest common divisor of n^5 - n and n^7 - n is a multiple of 30.
The product n(n^2-1) divides the gcd of n^5-n and n^7-n.
predicate: (n == 0 or n == 1 or n == -1) or (gcd(abs(pow(n, 5) - n), abs(pow(n, 7) - n)) % (abs(n*(n**2-1))) == 0)

claim: If p is a prime factor of n^2+1, then p is not 3.
For any integer n, n^2 + 1 is never divisible by 3.
The expression n^2 + n + 1 is never divisible by 2.
The greatest common divisor of n^2 + 1 and n^2 + n + 1 divides n.
If n is prime and n != 5, then n divides 5^(n-1) - 1.
predicate: (not is_prime(n) or n == 5) or (pow(5, n-1, n) == 1)

claim: If n is a prime congruent to 1 mod 4, then n is a sum of two squares.
predicate: (not (is_prime(n) and n % 4 == 1)) or any(x*x + y*y == n for x in range(int(n**0.5)+1) for y in range(int(n**0.5)+1))

claim: Every prime factor of n^2 + 1 is either 2 or of the form 4k + 1.
predicate: all(p == 2 or p % 4 == 1 for p in range(2, 500) if is_prime(p) and (n**2 + 1) % p == 0)

claim: If n is a multiple of 3, then n^2 + 1 has no factors of the form 3k + 1.
predicate: (n % 3 != 0) or all(p % 3 != 1 for p in range(2, 100) if is_prime(p) and (n**2 + 1) % p == 0)

claim: For all integers n, n^13 - n is divisible by 13.
For all integers n, n^13 - n is divisible by 2730.
The denominator of the n-th Bernoulli number B_n is related to the primality of n+1.
predicate: all(not is_prime(p) or (2730 % p == 0) for p in [2,3,5,7,13])

claim: If n is prime and n > 5, then n^4 is congruent to 1 mod 30.
If p is a prime > 5, then p^8 - 1 is divisible by 240.
If p is prime > 5, p^4 - 1 is divisible by 120 (revisited).
phi(n) is always even for n > 2.
If n > 2, the number of primitive roots modulo n is either 0 or even.
If n is a power of 5, phi(n) is divisible by 4.
If n = 5^k, then tau(n) = k+1.
The sum of the first n integers squared is n(n+1)(2n+1)/6.
predicate: 6 * sum(i*i for i in range(abs(n)+1)) == abs(n)*(abs(n)+1)*(2*abs(n)+1)

claim: The sum of the first n squares is always an integer.
The sum of the first n squares is divisible by 5 if n is a multiple of 5.
For n = 5k, the sum of the first n squares ends in the same digit as k or k+5.
predicate: (n <= 0 or n % 5 != 0) or ((sum(i*i for i in range(n+1))) % 5 == 0)

claim: For all n, n^10 - n^6 is divisible by 10.
For all n, n^13 - n^9 is divisible by 10.
The last digit of n^k repeats every 4 powers for k > 0.
predicate: all(pow(n, k+4, 10) == pow(n, k, 10) for k in range(1, 10))

claim: The sequence of last digits of n^k has period dividing 4.
If n is not divisible by 2 or 5, n^100 ends in 01.
For n coprime to 10, n^phi(100) is 1 mod 100.
The last two digits of n^40 are 01 if gcd(n, 10) = 1.
The least common multiple of {30, 42, 13} is a divisor of n^1561 - n.
